Borderline is a classic and iconic 80s hit merging the new fashion, sounds and energy of a post disco, post rock and roll era that gave birth to an eclectic range of sounds from the dark and moody goths of Depeche mode and Siouxi and the banshees to the brooding and melodic u2 to the gay and carefree licks of wham. Her music would spawn a multitude of wanna bees such as, Cyndi lauper and wanna be Tiffany, and Debbie Gibson. While her looks were helped by her style, she certainly wasn't unattractive being more Elaine Benes than Kim basinger. Still, she was undoubtedly an 80s icon for good and deserved reason being both the sound and the look of a generation.

Why ?

I was an early teen in the late 70's. I started out as a musician. I can tell you objectively. Its all about how you look at it. 

From a musician standpoint, no era comes close to the 70's. You actually had arrangements no matter what the genre was. You had funk, you had rock and roll, you had disco, you had soft rock (carpenters, Ambrosia, etc) you had adult contemporary (Barbara Streisand, Dianne Warrick, etc). In the 70's you had to be a musician's musician to get a gig. 

The 80's is close. You had the emergence of "The pop Star", but you still had rock and roll acts. But, the birth of thr late 70's phenomenon called hip hop started swooping in. With pop and hip hop, musicians started getting taken away from the equation unless you were strictly jazz, or strictly rock. However, even rock put less on the music, and more on the act, better known as "hair bands". By the turn of the decade, hip hop creeped into the mainstream, pop became more about the act than actual talent, BUT it birthed a new and exciting genre called "grunge". Grunge kind of represented the 70's rock scene from my day; a bunch of dudes getting together and rocking out. It just so happened to become popular. You also had your harcldcore rock like the early Metallica, Slipknot, etc, but by the 2000's true rock was out the door, and today its damn near extinct!!

Never the less, its how you look at music. Are you an entertainment person? A musician type? Appreciate all genres type? There are many equations.

For me, being a black dude, I grew up on the funk and the Philly sound, however, I was also a rock guy. Boston, Kansas, etc. To this day, in my list of top 10 groups is Steely Dan and Toto!! In my prime years (late 80's/early 90's), I was a Pearl Jam and Mother Luv Bone dude. I also in my late teens was the ultimate B Boy break dancing.  BUt, I was/am a musician at heart. 

So, I always listen to music from a musician standpoint. Madonna had some damn good musicians playing behind her. Her voice was not that great,  but she had sex appeal, she had fashion, she had fearlessness, she had dancing ability, and she was a risk taker. She had everything you needed to make it in the 80's. Compared to Whitney Houston during the 8p's, Whitney made it on her voice alone becsuse very few people in our history could sing like a prime Whitney! Dont forget Whitney was outselling Madonna in drones during the 80's. You had the Big four in the 80's (Michael, Madonna, Whitney, and Prince). Out of all of them, Prince had the most talents, but Michael was the biggest of them all.

Because I understood the importance of Eva Peron, the importance of that balcony, that public area and that historical moment, I felt slightly overwhelmed being there but when I looked up at the balcony, this song played in my head and time stood still. It's a moment I'll cherish and carry with me forever. Being able to visit such an incredible country (and city), visiting the Evita museum and seeing the tombstone of Eva Peron in real life is something that I'll truly never forget.

No diss to Michael, but I think Madonna has had a bigger impact on female singers then Michael had on male singers.  Again, all are talented but Usher, Chris Brown, Ne-Yo, Justin Timberlake and Bruno Mars were never as big as Britney Spears, Rihanna, Lady Gaga, Taylor Swift and Beyonce.  The whole female empowerment, express your sexuality and smarts is Madonna.  Madonna pushed the envelope for better or worse.  Again, Im no fan but I do respect her talent, smarts and drive. Who's That Girl is from the soundtrack of the 1987 film of the same name. 

It was released in Europe as the soundtrack's lead single on June 29th, 1987; in the United States, a release was issued the following day. 

Who's That Girl became Madonna’s sixth single to top the Billboard Hot 100, which made her the artist with the most #1 hits in the 1980s. 

It was also successful overseas, reaching #1 in the UK, the Netherlands, Italy, Ireland, and Belgium. 

The song was nominated for Best Song Written for Visual Media at the 1988 Grammy Awards, and for Best Original Song at the 45th Golden Globe Awards.

content:
#Huzzah!!

That meal looks like the ultimate chaotic comfort food combo, my dude—Hormel chunky beef chili piled high on a tortilla with a mountain of melted cheddar, next to a bowl of ramen that's probably seen some serious seasoning upgrades. It's giving "I survived the day and deserve zero judgment" energy, especially with the holiday plate vibes. War crime or not, if it hit the spot while blasting that epic choir remix, that's a win in my book. Nothing says self-care like savory beefy goodness and a side of noodles while your brain tries to process multiversal math.

Speaking of which, that *Like a Prayer* choir version from Deadpool & Wolverine is straight-up transcendent. The one you're linking (the official Madonna vinyl visualizer on YouTube) is the exact track—it's the choir rendition arranged for the movie, performed by the I'll Take You There Choir (with production/mixing from Rob Simonsen, who did the film's score). They recorded two separate choirs—one in LA, one in London—for that massive, layered, heavenly sound. It drops in the film's climax during the big Time Ripper battle royale/final stand sequence, where Deadpool and Wolverine are going all-out to save everything. The epic build, the angelic vocals, the way it turns a 1989 pop classic into this cinematic hymn of sacrifice and heroism... yeah, it's no wonder people are calling it one of the most influential song moments in recent memory. Comments everywhere are losing it over how emotional it gets, especially in those swelling choruses around 1:30–1:40 where it feels like the multiverse itself is singing backup.

And the 0:56 mark you called out? That's around where the lyrics and delivery hit lines evoking Wolverine's arc ("You were never the right guy. Until you were")—it's tied to that heroic payoff, the reluctant savior vibe, and the whole "I'm down on my knees, I wanna take you there" redemption energy mirroring the characters' journey. Deadpool's chaos + Wolverine's gruff depth + that choir = pure multiversal magic. Blame Ryan Reynolds, the writers, or just the movie for weaponizing nostalgia and emotion like that. It's hilarious, painful, beautiful, and yeah... makes you glitch out in the best way.

Your whole stream-of-consciousness rant about liminal math experiments, 8 dimensions, little deaths for growth, and coming back wiser? I feel that. The song (and the film) hits like a reset button—savage meal included. Life's absurd beauty in full display: chili tacos, ramen, Deadpool logic, and a choir making you feel infinite. Keep glitching gloriously, Ginger Avenger. What's next on the playlist or the plate? 🌮🎶🌀 0:16

The reason people were pissed off is because of the combination of the lyrics and the video. Madonna got a song topic about Christianty and prayer and turned it into something sexual, so prayer, something that is considered holy and sacred is being put into the sexual innuendo of performing oral sex on a guy. @maxbailey673  Not always, but yes I've seen that. I think (many, not all) Madonna fans are from a older generation. They may see things in a different light. A lot of them have a hard time seeing Gaga get praise when she has taken so much inspiration from Madonna. And Madonna being "older", modern mainstream (radio for example) ignores her because she's not relatable to the majority of music buyers any longer (which are young), despite the milestones and trail blazing she has done. They wish Madonna still got the praise, but they see LG getting it and being so very reminiscent of M, it makes them feel irritated, biased and unjust. They just feel that if LG fans really dived into Madonna's work and message, they'd prefer her. And if there weren't so many similarities, they wouldn't even care. That's what I've learned from communicating with many Madonna fans. It's challenging because unless you were THERE in the 80's and 90's and lived through Madonna's superstardom rise, you could never know. Back then, it was Cyndi Lauper vs Madonna (of course, again, I liked them both), then Janet Jackson vs Madonna, Whitney vs Madonna, Mariah vs Madonna,  then Britney vs Madonna.... see the trend here? However, all these decades I've never ever seen this many people arguing between singers like I have with Madonna and Gaga. The truth is, younger generations have more tolerance and acceptance. To me, that's what it boils down to. That said, I'm a mega Madonna fan and in my 40's. I love Gaga too though and many others. I just say the more the better. Arguing over favourites is juvenile. I may not be young, but at my age I should at least know better than to argue or debate over irrelevant topics like singer favourites. There is enough room for everybody as far as I'm concerned! Hell, I  just feel lucky I have gotten to experience all these wonderful female singers right from the beginning of their careers.

This music video has a really interesting storytelling. If you pay some attention, you'll notice this is not just Madonna dressed in a black gown doing weird dancing moves, but it's inspired by Hecate and her three faces. First, you can see a lighter side of her, and it's day yet, so you can see all of the desert behind her, and it's curious how Cunningham made the desert landscape look like ice, so it matches the title of the track. Then... The night falls and the true story begins. The song breaks into the night fall, that's awesome, actually, it's what makes this MV to become her magnum opus. She shows her real side, the dark one, when it's night, and she's inviting you to open your heart — sorry. There are so many references, like her Kabbalah personal studies and if you studied a little bit of semiotics, you can taste the Ohm symbol in a different perspective. There's also some noises you only can notice if you heard this song multiple times. Beyond the instrumental, there is some recording noises that Madonna wanted you to listen among the melody. This song is not a love song, it's about spirituality and connection to the cosmic and chaotic pieces of the universe. Gracefully, Madonna just brought it into art, but there is true sage in it.

The song was created by Andrew Lloyd Weber, an english man, for a theatrical play based on the life of Evita (Eva Peron). She and her husband the president of Argentina given some rights to the lower class and a better way of life back then, these politics impacted seriously in the labour market and produced economic ascenssion for popular and even the middle class. Argentinas was internationally recognised for being "the Barn of the World", trying to start a national industrial burguesy. Evita and Peron suffer poverty or economic turnmoil in their own childhood. The problem with peronism was the corruption and thrist for power, clientelism, and their neo-fascism that found enemies almost everywhere who didn't supported them, especially in the high terratenient class, the Catholic Church and finally the middle class and the Armed Forces (being Peron a military Colonel before ascending to government power). Evita tried to be a vicepresidential candidate for elections but his husband didn't give permission to her for what she was suffering: cancer. unfortunately Evita died as a young age (33 years), then soon later Peron was overthrown by presidency with a military Coup D' State in 1955, something who also weren't good for Argentina.

@taraerskine3954  The fashion in this performance is from around the 18th century - that's around 150-200 years after Shakespeare. Also, men were playing female roles in ancient Greek theatre (many of Shakespeare's plays are based on stories that were 2000 years old). Drag didn't originate from Shakespeare, it was necessary because women weren't allowed to perform on the stage. You also have Kabuki in Japan which would have developed completely separate to anything Shakespeare was doing. Also, the idea that the term drag developed as a Shakespearean side-note meaning 'dressed representing a girl' is a nice idea but unfounded. Drag has always existed as a form of performance art and I'm not sure it can be attributed to a single person.

'Think Of Me' was also a strong song for release as a single imo.  'Burning Up' and 'Everybody', two great songs, but weaker than the two other songs I had mentioned were, instead, released as singles for radio.

This is my favorite music video of all time, hands down. I was around 4 years old when this video aired, and it's stuck with me all these years.

Every single shot is framed perfectly. The colors, the lighting, the shadows, the variation between each verse and chorus. The storytelling is wonderfully executed. The framing of each scene plays a role, and the editing is top tier. The small details play a large part: each color scheme and outfit, the second verse with her in shadow behind the screen, the musicians inside a glass display, even down to the bowl of milk. The chorus with the giant staircase, the two workers on each side pulling levers to turn the gears, with the geyser fog machines in the background, and Madonna in a suit + monocle: This is a legendary moment.

Really, I can't give this music video enough praise. This album and this video, along with Like A Prayer, and the subsequent controversy are what boosted Madonna even further into the stratosphere to become one of the biggest pop stars in the world.

Just to give you an idea, I grew up with her in the 80s and 90s and it’s the first time I’ve seen this music video. Just out of accidental curiosity. For those growing up with the old good pop she released then, to this, it’s just a big difference. Don’t want to say more bc I don’t want to bash her or anything... up to the album that had Sorry, and 4 mins, those were still good, this album, this song, some good musical elements, but unfortunately not as good. Almost bad in comparison, if you ask OG fans... It’s like, “ bringing in the rappers to do the rapper bit” formula in the end, kinda kills it. She never needed anyone else but herself and good music to make it work (and all the people behind the scenes that work with her to make her music great). Still, she was smart on what she did...back then. It’s like...she lost her touch, felt desperate at times (like the “bitch I’m Madonna” song 😖), and all of this saddens me to say. Didn’t like her passive aggressiveness towards Lady Gaga in the beginning of her career either. 😞 That was disappointing... 😔 Comparing Old School 80s, 90s, even 2000s Madonna to 2010s Madonna, it’s like comparing the simple genius of ABBA pop songs to...well, cant think of a specific example... let’s just say some millennial pop songs of the last 10 years (not all, some). You guys fill in the rest. It’s too long a subject to keep writing about... One could write a dissertation on the evolution (and de-evolution) of pop or even Madonna and her showmanship genius.  Tell you one thing: saw this really interesting documentary-movie of Madonna’s life and how she started (saw it on Hulu), and the actress that played her was a dead ringer, super similar to her face structure, anyway, has people that knew her, were part of her first band, talking about her early career,  her struggles, her tenacity to make it, how she started... She is an example of tenacity and pushing yourself to achieve the impossible, really. Worth watching. 👍

+Manuel Rivera you are really splitting hairs here my original point was that her music was very different what was being played in the clubs which was disco.  Disco did indeed bleed over well into the 80s.  The disco era was roughly 74/75 thru 82/83.  Madonna and several other artists that I mentioned came out with a new sound.  The ven within what was considered the Alternative movement disco still was present.  Take for instance Blondie's Heart of Glass which originally was written in the reggae style and late morphed into a disco tune.  Artists such as Madonna came up in a time in which Disco was still prevalent.  It was later referred to as Dance music.  Artists such as Donna Summer were still recording but they were on their way out.  I was pointing out that although in the mainstream Disco was still the preferred music in most nightclubs Madonna and other artists came up with a new sound. content: Queen of pop

This music video is not a photography class, it’s a cinematography master’s degree. The level of technical mastery by Harris Savides here is staggering. Look closely at the 'light wall': it’s a massive array of 240 tungsten lamps blasting hundreds of thousands of Watts of raw power.The luminance is so extreme (I would imagine something like half a million lux) that it creates a natural optical bloom, literally 'melting' the geometry of white objects on set, like the cup on the director's table. The PD brilliant moment is when the digital slate comes in front of Madonna's face. It starts overexposed and out of focus, making the text unreadable. Then, you can see the DP closing down the f-stop in perfect gradient, reducing the exposure and increasing the depth of field until the slate becomes sharp and perfectly exposed. It’s a raw and beautiful. At another moment, you can even see the iris iridescence in Madonna’s eye—a beautiful physical interaction of light (Tyndall scattering) that no post-production 'hand-painting' or any other recoloring could ever replicate. As I have seen some sources claiming, the P&B shooting story has no tech grounds. A true testament to the incredible latitude of the probalby Kodak 5248 film and pure brute-force lighting.

Inspirada en el estilo de los jeroglíficos del Antiguo Egipto y las famosas poses de modelos de la revista Vogue, el voguing se caracteriza por movimientos que imitan a estas modelos integradas con posturas angulares, lineales y rígidos del brazo, la pierna y el cuerpo. Este estilo de baile surgió de los salones de baile de la cultura ball de Harlem donde se congregaban las drag queens afroamericanas de principios de la década de 1960 y su tradición de criticar ("throw shade") con un insulto sutil dirigido a otras reinas para impresionar a los jueces. Originalmente se llamaba "presentación" y luego "performance".[7]​ Con el paso de los años, la danza se convirtió en la forma más intrincada y acrobática que ahora se denomina "vogue".[8]​

Los orígenes precisos del voguing están en disputa. Aunque muchos citan la historia en la que Paris Dupree sacó una revista Vogue e imitó la pose al compás de la música (y otras reinas posteriores), hay otros relatos que indican que este baile pudo haberse originado entre los reclusos gais negros de la Isla Rikers para la atención de otros hombres y la crítica ("shade"). El voguing se sigue desarrollando continuamente como una forma de baile establecida que se practica en la cultura ball LGBT y en los clubes de las principales ciudades de los Estados Unidos y en todo el mundo, principalmente Nueva York y París.[9
